title: [epic] dune-config: composable, variant-aware DUNE Jsonnet config package
description:
Create devel/dune-config, a standalone Jsonnet configuration package, by porting/translating devel/wire-cell-phlex/cfg/dune into a structure that scales to many detectors and job variants.

Two strictly-separate config domains in one package:
  - phlex/  : phlex/phlexed job configuration (references the WCT job-config file it feeds).
  - wct/    : wire-cell-toolkit configuration, factored into job-level (detname-parameterized) and detector-level (detname-selected) concerns.

Three architectural decisions (settled 2026-06-29):
  1. Location: devel/dune-config (a buildable/installable source pkg, like wire-cell-phlex/cfg, placed on the phlex/WIRECELL search path via spack develop). NOT extern/repos (that holds Spack recipe repos).
  2. Subgraph composition: adopt the WCT pgraph.jsonnet pnode library (pnode/intern/pipeline/components/fan.*, available at share/wirecell/pgraph.jsonnet). sim-sigproc becomes pg.pipeline([sim, sigproc]); per-anode assembly becomes pg.fan.*; this removes the hand-written-edge duplication seen in cfg/dune/wct/job/sim-sigproc.jsonnet (322 lines ~= sim + sigproc rewritten).
  3. Detector variants: single canonical detname + a "variant" param selecting an overlay merged onto the base detector (base + patch). The job->detector contract stays "select by detname"; variant is just another param. Enforced via a detector schema + validate().

Detectors span the 2x2 matrix (FD,PD) x (VD,HD) plus geometry phases; each cell is a detector family dir, phases/ideal/real are variant overlays.

Milestones (in order):
  M1 Scaffold pkg + core libs (pgraph conventions, schema/variant resolver).
  M2 Port PDHD/PDVD detectors + WCT sim/sigproc subgraphs; achieve config parity with cfg/dune and dunereco refs.
  M3 Swappable SP input: simulation <-> raw DUNE-DAQ files (sim path live now; DAQ provider gated on Phlex 0.3.0 source/driver + resources APIs, ddm-66h/ddm-999/ddm-3j8.1.6).
  M4 Demonstrate PDHD as-built ("real", one pathological APA) variant.
  M5 Expand to FD detectors by porting reference/dunereco/dunereco/DUNEWireCell.

Reference (read-only, do not depend): reference/dunereco/dunereco/DUNEWireCell/{common,pdhd,protodunevd,dune10kt-*,dune-vd*}. Authoritative for parameter values.
Source to port: devel/wire-cell-phlex/cfg/dune/ and its docs/dune-config.md.

title: End-to-end channel-map validation on real PDHD/PDVD fragments + channel-arg convention
description:
Confirm the FULL chain produces physically-correct OFFLINE channel ids on real data: real fragment -> decode (local channel c) -> OnlineOfflineChannelMap.offline(det,crate,slot,stream,c) -> offline id. Today we have validated the pieces but NOT the join:
- decode ADC values vs WIBEthFrame::get_adc (ddm-3j8.1.8 golden) DONE
- map PARSER vs real vendored tables, first-row self-consistency (ddm-3j8.1.12) DONE
- NOT DONE: that the decoder's local channel index c, fed through the chosen map, yields the correct offline channel for a real PDHD and a real PDVD fragment.

KEY OPEN ISSUE - channel-argument convention: our WIBEth decoder emits c = 0..63 (= streamchan), which matches the 12-col WARM maps (PD2VDTPCChannelMap, streamchan 0..63). But the 13-col COLD maps (PD2HD, PD2VDBottom) key on wibframechan 0..255 - a DIFFERENT numbering, not a rescale. Must determine, per detector/fragment frame-format, which map + which channel index pairs correctly. In particular: do our real PDHD WIBEth fragments map through a 13-col (wibframechan) table, or is there a 64-channel convention? Resolve before trusting cold-map output.

Also: pick the correct map VERSION per detector/run for our actual data files (PDVD np02vd run040380; the PDHD runs) and document the defaults. Cross-check a handful of channels against duneprototypes (offline) expectations if available as an independent reference (read-only, no dependency).
acceptance_criteria: For one real PDHD and one real PDVD fragment: the correct vendored map+version is identified; a known online channel resolves to the expected offline id end-to-end; the streamchan-vs-wibframechan convention is documented and applied correctly per frame-format; wc.frame traces carry verified offline ids.
notes:
Split out of ddm-3j8.1.12 (which covered sourcing+parser+bridge wiring). This is the remaining CORRECTNESS validation. Needs real data files (data/PDHD, data/PDVD; not committed). Relates ddm-3j8.1.12, ddm-3j8.1.13, ddm-3j8.1.5, ddm-3j8.1.8.
PROGRESS (2026-06-22, dune-daq-codec 42f2658): MAP GOLDEN done. Discovered DUNE's dune::TPCChannelMapSP (12-col) and dune::PD2HDChannelMapSP (13-col) are framework-free standalone classes (plain stdlib, ns 'dune' — NOT the cetlib/ers/logging plugin base), so usable as a no-DUNE-stack oracle exactly like WIBEthFrame. tools/gen_golden_chanmap.cpp (dev-only, links reference SP .cpp) emits committed golden fixtures; reference-free test_channel_map_golden checks OnlineOfflineChannels vs DUNE. Validated EXHAUSTIVELY: 22528 keys (12288 PD2VDTPCChannelMap_v2 warm + 10240 PD2HDChannelMap_v6 cold), 0 mismatches, incl. cold wib=slot+1. => our loader is bit-identical to DUNE's authoritative map. ctest 6/6.

STILL OPEN (the harder half): (1) end-to-end on a REAL fragment: HDF5->decode->map->offline, confirm physically-correct ids (needs single-TR data, user extracting); (2) channel-arg convention: WIBEth decoder emits c=0..63=streamchan (matches 12-col warm). Must determine whether real PDHD WIBEth data maps via a 12-col or 13-col table and, if cold, the streamchan(0..63)->wibframechan(0..255) relation. Loader correctness is now settled; the convention/version-selection is what the visual + end-to-end check will resolve.
TOOL BUILT (2026-06-22): new package source/dune-daq-arrow, commit 2b0e3af. daq_hdf_to_arrow runs the full non-Phlex chain DAQ-HDF5 -> dune-daq-hdf -> codec -> OnlineOfflineChannelMap -> dune-daq-wct(IFrame) -> wire-cell-arrow(to_arrow_sparse) -> arrow-hdf, writing the SAME wc.frame HDF5 the Phlex pipeline produces (type wc.frame; members traces/frame_tags/trace_tags/cmm), 1 frame per trigger record. Reports an UNMAPPED-channel count (offline id<0) as a map diagnostic. Single-TR extraction via h5copy (documented in README).

FIRST RESULT / convention gap quantified: PDHD np04hd run027980 TR00008 with the 13-col cold map PD2HDChannelMap_v6 -> 10240 traces from 160 WIBEth fragments, valid wc.frame written, but 7680/10240 (75%) UNMAPPED. Confirms WIBEth decode index c=0..63 (streamchan) does NOT map through a cold map's wibframechan(0..255); only coincidental low-index overlaps hit. So for WIBEth either (a) the right table is a 12-col warm/streamchan map, or (b) a (link,streamchan)->wibframechan transform is needed for cold maps. NEXT: run on PDVD with the warm map (expect ~0 unmapped) to validate the warm path end-to-end, and use the user's offline-channel-vs-tick display on the output to confirm coherent tracks. Pending user: single-TR extraction + display support for wc.frame.
CRITICAL BUG found+fixed via the visual pipeline (2026-06-22). dune-daq-hdf read_bytes used H5Dread with H5T_NATIVE_UINT8 on the SIGNED int8 (H5T_STD_I8) fragment datasets -> HDF5 clamped bytes 0x80-0xFF to 0, zeroing ~half of every payload. Decoded waveforms were a mix of physical + round-in-hex garbage (0,0x1C00,0x1F00,0x2000). Invisible to all prior cross-checks because they sourced bytes through the same read_bytes (our decode == get_adc, both fed clamped bytes); an INDEPENDENT h5py read exposed it. Fix (dune-daq-hdf 4224059): read with the dataset's own datatype (H5Dget_type) = verbatim copy; regression test writes signed int8 0x00-0xFF and checks round-trip. WIBEth golden fixtures regenerated (dune-daq-codec 8f6409c). Verified end-to-end: full pipeline waveforms now physical (chan 191: 8153 8150 8152 8156... ; fraction |dtick|>500 went 0.217 -> 0.0000). NOTE: channel-map convention/unmapped issue (75% UNMAPPED with cold map on WIBEth) is SEPARATE and still open.
RESOLVED for cold/HD (2026-06-22, dune-daq-codec 2ccfa37). Root cause was your hypothesis #2: the DAQEthHeader stream_id is COMPOSITE, not a flat online channel id. Confirmed against DUNE's own PD2HDChannelMapSPPluginBase::get_offline_channel_from_det_crate_slot_stream_chan: link=(stream>>6)&1, wibframechan=64*(stream&0x3)+channel (channel=WIBEth frame-local 0..63), invalid unless stream in {0..3,64..67}. Our OnlineOfflineChannels cold branch had treated stream as link / channel as wibframechan, so only stream 0,1 matched a valid link -> 2560/10240 mapped to a sparse scrambled offline set. Fixed offline() to do the decomposition (now truly mirrors DUNE's unified interface; warm/VD path unchanged = pass-through). Cold golden regenerated to unified keys; still validates vs DUNE. ctest 6/6. END-TO-END on real PDHD run027980 record0: all 10240 channels -> 10240 distinct offline ids 0..10239 (full 4-APA detector), 0 unmapped, emitted in ascending offline order. NOTE: file holds 4 APAs (crate 1-4), not 1 as earlier assumed. REMAINING: user visual confirmation of coherent UVW tracks; PDVD end-to-end (warm top + cold bottom) validation.

title: CRITICAL: online->offline channel map for DUNE DAQ decode
description:
The DUNE DAQ ADC data identifies channels by an ONLINE key — (det_id, crate_id, slot_id, stream_id) from the DAQEthHeader plus the local channel index (0..63) within a WIBEth/TDEEth frame. The offline software (and the Arrow wc.frame we produce) MUST use OFFLINE channel IDs. So the bridge (ddm-3j8.1.5) requires an authoritative online->offline CHANNEL MAP. It is detector-specific (PDHD and PDVD differ; PDS differs again).

NOT FOUND in the reference clones (no detchannelmaps, no mapping logic in daqdataformats/detdataformats/fddetdataformats/hdf5libs, none in WCT source) -> the authoritative map must be sourced (DUNE 'detchannelmaps' package, a per-detector map table/CSV, or a WCT channel-map service). USER ACTION: track down the definitive map for PDHD/PDVD (TPC + PDS).

Until then, ddm-3j8.1.5 ships a clearly-marked PLACEHOLDER (identity/composed online id) behind a ChannelMap interface; ALL channel IDs flow through that interface, so installing the real map is a localized change. This issue = obtain + implement the real map (likely a new pure 'dune-daq-channelmap' package or a data table + loader, reimplemented zero-dependency per project rule) and wire it into the bridge. CRITICAL: Arrow output with online IDs would be wrong.
acceptance_criteria:
- An authoritative online(det,crate,slot,stream,local_channel)->offline mapping for PDHD and PDVD (TPC at least), sourced and documented.
- A ChannelMap implementation the bridge uses in place of the placeholder; verified against a known channel on real data.
- The wc.frame traces carry OFFLINE channel IDs.
notes:
FOUND (2026-06-18): reference/detchannelmaps (DUNE-DAQ) and reference/duneprototypes (offline) both carry the SAME authoritative map .txt tables. KEY INTERFACE (reference/detchannelmaps/include/detchannelmaps/TPCChannelMap.hpp) matches our ChannelMap 1:1:
  uint get_offline_channel_from_det_crate_slot_stream_chan(det, crate, slot, stream, channel)
WHO CONVERTS: detchannelmaps is a shared LOOKUP library (electronics IDs -> offline channel). DAQ ships it because online code (trigger-primitive generation) needs offline channel numbers, but the persisted raw ADC fragments stay keyed by ELECTRONICS coords (det/crate/slot/stream/channel in DAQEthHeader + the WIBEth/TDEEth frame's local channel). DAQ does NOT pre-convert stored ADC to offline order -> WE must do the lookup. (Confirms the original assumption.)

OUR DECODE KEY -> map key: det=DAQEthHeader.det_id, crate=crate_id, slot=slot_id, stream=stream_id, channel/streamchan = local channel 0..63 (= WIBEthFrame channel = wibframechan).

COLUMN MEANINGS:
- Generic TPCChannelMapSP (PDVD; config/pd2vd/PD2VDTPCChannelMap_v2.txt, 12 cols): offlchan detid detelement crate slot stream streamchan ... ; map key = (detid,crate,slot,stream,streamchan)->offlchan. For TDE: slot=card, stream=0, streamchan=channel (so PDVD TOP TDEEth uses PD2VDTopTDEChannelMap).
- PD2HD (config/pd2hd/PD2HDChannelMap_v3.txt, 13 cols): offlchan crate APAName wib link femb_on_link cebchan plane chan_in_plane femb asic asicchan wibframechan; key (crate, wib=slot+1, link=stream, wibframechan=channel)->offlchan.
File path resolved via env DETCHANNELMAPS_SHARE -> <share>/config/<det>/<file>.

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN (pure, zero-dep): a dune-daq-channelmap package that (a) VENDORS the relevant .txt tables (PD2HDChannelMap_v3, PD2VDTPCChannelMap_v2, PD2VDTopTDEChannelMap_*) as committed data with provenance (identical in detchannelmaps + duneprototypes = authoritative); (b) reimplements the loader + offline(det,crate,slot,stream,channel)->offlchan lookup; (c) a ChannelMap impl for dune-daq-wct (drop-in for IdentityChannelMap). No DUNE build, no detchannelmaps link. OPEN DETAIL: pick the map VERSION matching each run era (plugins default to PD2VDTPCChannelMap_v2 / PD2HD v1or3); confirm against our data runs.
DONE (2026-06-22, dune-daq-codec commit 3f5a123 + cc8050b/9e8931f): (1) real maps SOURCED+VENDORED from detchannelmaps@bf467ea into data/channelmaps/{pd2hd,pd2vd} with PROVENANCE.md, installed to share/dune_daq_codec/channelmaps; (2) reimplemented zero-dep loader = OnlineOfflineChannels (auto-detects 12-col warm / 13-col cold); (3) wired into bridge = OnlineOfflineChannelMap replaces IdentityChannelMap; (4) parser validated against real vendored tables (first-row online->offline self-consistency, ctest 5/5). SPLIT OUT (remaining work, not closing-blockers for the 'obtain+implement+wire' deliverable): channel-arg convention (streamchan 0-63 vs wibframechan 0-255) + end-to-end physical validation on real fragments -> ddm-3j8.1.16; relative-path search-path resolution -> ddm-3j8.1.15; Phlex resource delivery -> ddm-3j8.1.14. RECOMMEND: close this issue; tracking continues in .16/.15/.14.
close_reason: Real PDHD/PDVD maps sourced+vendored (detchannelmaps@bf467ea -> data/channelmaps, PROVENANCE.md), zero-dep loader OnlineOfflineChannels (auto-detects 12/13-col), wired into the bridge as OnlineOfflineChannelMap (replaces IdentityChannelMap), parser validated on real tables (ctest 5/5). Remaining work broken out: end-to-end physical validation + channel-arg convention -> ddm-3j8.1.16; search-path resolution -> ddm-3j8.1.15; Phlex resource delivery -> ddm-3j8.1.14.

title: phlex-arrow-common: structured (cell/creator/product) Address mapping
description:
Capture the Phlex-structured addressing being REMOVED from arrow-hdf (ddm-c3s.1) so arrow-hdf stays domain/framework-neutral. arrow-hdf's Address is now a flat path: Address(std::vector<std::string> components) (escapes each via path_escape) or Address(std::string path) (verbatim), with path()/group_path(). The Phlex-aware structure that builds those components must live in the Phlex glue (phlex-arrow-common per design doc section 5 'store -> address mapping'; this realizes part of ddm-c3s.2). Possible alternative home: phlex-arrow-hdf (per a request) — confirm; design favors common since it is tech-agnostic (identical for rntuple/parquet).

## Concept removed from arrow-hdf (to re-implement here)
The old API was:
  struct Cell { std::string layer; int64_t number; };
  Address(std::vector<Cell> cells, std::string creator, std::string product);
  // path()       -> /esc(layer0)/number0/.../esc(creator)/esc(product)
  // group_path() -> /esc(layer0)/number0/...   (the cell path, no creator/product)
Cells map a Phlex store.index() (data_cell_index of (layer_name, number) pairs); creator = store.source(); product = the product suffix. Numbers render as plain decimal; non-numeric components are percent-escaped (forbid '/' and NUL).

## To do (in the Phlex glue)
Provide a helper that builds an arrow_hdf::Address from (cells, creator, product):
  components = [layer0, to_string(number0), ..., creator, product]
  return arrow_hdf::Address(components)   // arrow-hdf escapes + joins
i.e. arrow-hdf owns escaping/joining; the Phlex layer owns the cell/creator/product STRUCTURE. Round-trip (path -> structured) belongs to the read-side hierarchy issue.

## References
phlex-file-io-design.md sections 5,6. arrow-hdf Address.h (post-refactor). Blocked-by ddm-c3s.1 (done).
close_reason: Implemented in new package source/phlex-arrow-common (phlex_arrow_common/CellAddress.{h,cpp}). The structured (cell/creator/product) addressing removed from arrow-hdf is reimplemented here: Cell{layer,number} + creator + product mapped to a generic arrow_hdf::Address via make_address() (components -> arrow-hdf escapes/joins) and the inverse parse_address(). Pure (arrow-hdf only, no Phlex). Builds via find_package(arrow_hdf CONFIG); ctest test_cell_address 1/1 (round-trips incl. escaping/job-root/large-number + parse error cases). Home is phlex-arrow-common (confirmed by user; per design section 5). The Phlex-store extraction that produces (cells,creator,product) from a product_store is deliverable #1 of ddm-c3s.2.

title: phlex-arrow-hdf: HDF5 output module (write)
description:
Create the WRITE side of `phlex-arrow-hdf`: a thin Phlex output module that
persists Arrow products to HDF5 by delegating to `arrow-hdf`.

## Context

This is the thin per-technology Phlex glue for the HDF5 write path. The output
module is brief: it marshals selected Arrow products from each store and hands
them to `arrow-hdf`. Phlex plugin loading IS the technology dispatch (loading
this plugin registers the HDF5 output module) — do not add an internal backend
registry. See `phlex-file-io-design.md` (ddm umbrella top dir), section 6.

## Output module duties (per store received)

1. SELECT the Arrow-typed products matching configuration (delegate to the
   `phlex-arrow-common` selector).
2. ADDRESS each selected product: read the full structured `store.index()`,
   `store.source()` (creator), and product suffix; build the neutral `Address`
   (delegate to the `phlex-arrow-common` store->Address helper). This is where
   the structured hierarchy reaches HDF5 — avoiding FORM's index-flattening.
3. DELEGATE serialization to `arrow-hdf::write(table, address)`.
4. LAYER selection if configured: inspect `store.index()->layer_name()` and
   early-return, or use `.experimental_when(predicate)` gating.

Steps 1, 2, 4 are tech-agnostic (phlex-arrow-common); step 3 is the thin
delegation here. Keep the module body small.

## Concurrency

- Register the output module at `concurrency::serial` so its HDF5 writes are
  mutually exclusive in time (libhdf5 is not thread-safe by default). Document
  that raising concurrency requires a thread-safe libhdf5 build or internal
  locking. Distinct output modules run concurrently with each other; that is
  safe here because HDF5 is this module's exclusive technology in the job.

## Dependencies

- `arrow-hdf` (write path + Address type).
- `phlex-arrow-common` (write-side helpers).

## References

- `phlex-file-io-design.md` sections 6, 9.
- Phlex output registration: `sources/phlex/phlex/core/declared_output.*`,
  `make_computational_edges.cpp` (edges_to_outputs), and the existing
  `sources/phlex/form/form_module.cpp` for the registration pattern.

## Acceptance

- Loading the plugin registers an HDF5 output module.
- An integration test runs a small graph (source + a to_arrow transform + this
  output module) and produces an HDF5 file whose group path reflects the
  data-cell hierarchy and whose datasets match the Arrow schema.
notes: DONE + tested. Output module (committed earlier) + NEW full in-graph integration test (commit 5cda897): integration_frame_to_hdf.jsonnet drives the phlex binary over wcp_frame_source -> wire_cell_phlex_arrow_convert -> phlex_arrow_hdf_output; integration_write.sh asserts HDF5 layout (event/1,event/2 data-cell groups; 4 reserved wc.frame member groups each w/ __arrow_schema__; arrow.group.type='wc.frame' marker). ctest 2/2 (output_write + integration_write). Plugins dev-installed to /devel/ddm/install/lib. Runtime gotcha recorded: memory phlex-arrow-write-path-integration.
close_reason: HDF5 output module + full in-graph write-path integration test implemented and passing (ctest 2/2).

title: phlex-arrow-common: write-side + convert-node helpers
description:
Create `phlex-arrow-common`: a Phlex-dependent but technology-agnostic helper
library shared by all `phlex-arrow-<tech>` packages. THIS ISSUE covers the
WRITE-SIDE and conversion-node helpers only. (Read-side driver/provider helpers
are a separate, deferred issue.)

## Context

In the narrow-waist design, each technology gets a thin `phlex-arrow-<tech>`
Phlex package that delegates data serialization to a pure `arrow-<tech>` library.
But some logic is Phlex-aware (it touches `data_cell_index`, `product_store`)
yet technology-independent. Putting it here prevents each thin per-tech package
from duplicating it. See `phlex-file-io-design.md` (ddm umbrella top dir),
sections 5, 6.

## CRITICAL design caution

Do NOT invent abstractions that Phlex already provides. The existing FORM
prototype's mistake was an INTERNAL indirection to a technology backend
(its own `Placement`/`Token`/`IStorage` matrix) that both (a) duplicated what
Phlex's multi-output-module mechanism already does and (b) flattened away the
structured store index. The correct approach is multiple FIRST-CLASS Phlex
output modules / providers, each reading `store.index()` directly. This library
must be thin helpers only — no backend registry, no parallel dispatch mechanism
(Phlex plugin loading IS the dispatch). If a proposed abstraction reproduces
something Phlex offers, drop it.

## Deliverables (write-side)

1. **Store -> Address mapping**: given a `product_store` and a product, build the
   Phlex-neutral `Address` (sequence of `(layer_name, number)` from the full
   structured `data_cell_index`, plus creator from `store.source()` and the
   product suffix). The Address type itself is defined in `arrow-hdf` (or a tiny
   shared header) to stay Phlex-neutral; this helper populates it from Phlex
   types. This is the piece that fixes FORM's index-flattening defect.
2. **Arrow-product selection**: given a store and a configuration, select the
   Arrow-typed products this module should handle.
3. **Convert-node helper**: a generic helper/template to register a Phlex
   transform node that wraps a `to_arrow(...)` or `from_arrow(...)` converter
   function, so per-domain packages (e.g. wire-cell-phlex-arrow) only instantiate
   it for their concrete types. Both directions.

## Dependencies

- Depends on the neutral `Address` type and (optionally) the scan descriptor
  defined by `arrow-hdf`.
- Used by `wire-cell-phlex-arrow` and `phlex-arrow-hdf` (output module).

## References

- `phlex-file-io-design.md` sections 5, 6.
- Phlex handle/store API: `sources/phlex/phlex/model/handle.hpp`,
  `product_store.hpp`, `data_cell_index.hpp` (for `layer_name`/`number`/`parent`,
  `store.source()`, `store.index()`).

## Acceptance

- Helpers build against Phlex + Arrow.
- A unit test constructs a synthetic store with a multi-layer index and asserts
  the produced Address has the correct `(layer, number)` path, creator, and
  product.
- No technology-specific code; no backend registry abstraction.
notes: ALL deliverables done. #3 convert-node helper = phlex_arrow::register_convert (ConvertNode.h, commit 7a97e2a): generic register_convert(m,name,conv,query,suffix,conc) factoring the transform/input_family/output_product_suffixes boilerplate; direction-agnostic (any conv In->Out), so serves both to_arrow and from_arrow nodes. First consumer + validation: wire-cell-phlex-arrow (ddm-c3s.4, commit c855011). Header-only, pulls phlex/module.hpp (C++23, phlex::module); the C++20 common lib does not compile it. ctest still 3/3 (helper tested via c3s.4).
close_reason: wire-cell-phlex-arrow convert nodes + generic register_convert helper implemented, built, and round-trip tested; deps dev-installed.

title: arrow-hdf: generic Arrow <-> HDF5 serialization (pure)
description:
Create `arrow-hdf`: a pure (Phlex-free, WCT-free) C++ library that serializes
Apache Arrow objects to and from HDF5 files generically, driven by the Arrow
schema.

## Context

This is the M-axis "Arrow <-> technology" serializer for HDF5 in the narrow-waist
design. It must depend ONLY on Apache Arrow and HDF5 (and a small neutral
address type). It must NOT depend on Phlex or Wire-Cell Toolkit. See
`phlex-file-io-design.md` (ddm umbrella top dir), sections 5 and 6.

## Goal: HDF5 that is useful "in place"

A primary, BEST-EFFORT goal: the HDF5 representation should be directly useful to
users who are NOT using the arrow-hdf converters. For example, an array that
originated from a WCT `IFrame` should be recognizable as a set of waveforms that
a user can load with `h5py` (the Python HDF5 wrapper) as NumPy-like arrays,
without going back through Arrow. Concretely: map Arrow columns to
conventionally-shaped, typed HDF5 datasets; put Arrow field/schema metadata into
HDF5 attributes; prefer rectangular datasets where the schema allows.

There is real tension: some Arrow schemas (nested `list<...>`, `list<struct<...>>`,
variable-length data) do not map cleanly to a single rectangular HDF5 dataset.
For those, fall back to a faithful encoding (e.g. Arrow values+offsets buffers as
separate datasets, or HDF5 variable-length types) and document the convention.
"In-place usability" is best-effort, not a hard requirement, but should drive the
default layout decisions for the common (rectangular) cases.

## Deliverables

1. Write path: `write(arrow::Table, Address) -> HDF5`. Map the Arrow schema to
   HDF5 group/dataset/attribute structure. The `Address` is a Phlex-neutral
   structured path: a sequence of `(layer_name, number)` pairs plus creator and
   product names, used to build a group path like
   `/run/3/event/12/<creator>/<product>`. (Defining this neutral Address type is
   part of this issue; keep it free of Phlex/WCT.)
2. Read path: `read(Address) -> arrow::Table`, the inverse.
3. File scan: enumerate a file's stored addresses into a generic hierarchy
   descriptor (layer names + the cell tree of `(layer, number)` + per-cell
   product locations). This descriptor is consumed later by the Phlex read side;
   keep it Phlex-neutral here.
4. A documented schema -> HDF5 layout convention covering: rectangular columns,
   nested/list/struct columns, schema metadata -> attributes, and the opaque
   `large_binary` (blob) case.
5. CTest unit tests and round-trip tests (Arrow -> HDF5 -> Arrow equality).

## Design notes

- Sanitize every path component used as an HDF5 link name (forbid `/` and NUL;
  pick a portable charset/escaping).
- Decide numeric-vs-lexicographic ordering of numbered groups (zero-pad, or use
  HDF5 creation-order tracking) so readers can iterate cells in numeric order.
- Stores can land at ANY layer (including the job root), so support arbitrary
  address depth, not a fixed run/event shape.
- Weigh group-per-cell vs events-as-rows: a group per cell mirrors the hierarchy
  for browsing but, at large N, bloats HDF5 metadata and fights columnar
  efficiency. Make the grouping layer a deliberate, possibly configurable choice.
- libhdf5 thread-safety: document that callers must serialize access (the Phlex
  output node provides this); arrow-hdf itself need not lock internally but must
  state the contract.

## References

- `phlex-file-io-design.md` sections 4, 5, 6, 9, 10.
- Existing Arrow schemas in `sources/wire-cell-arrow/wire_cell_arrow/Converters.h`
  (e.g. `wc.frame`, `wc.depo`, `wc.tensor`) for representative inputs.

## Acceptance

- Library builds with only Arrow + HDF5 deps (no Phlex, no WCT).
- Round-trip tests pass for: a flat table (e.g. wc.tensor), a list-column table
  (wc.trace charge), and a nested list<struct> table (wc.depo priors).
- A `h5py` smoke check confirms a frame's waveform dataset is loadable as a
  NumPy array for the rectangular (dense) case.
notes:
COMPLETE 2026-06-16 (source/arrow-hdf, branch main, commits 53391f5 + 46f47d8). HDF5 C lib now in view (1.14.6). All deliverables + acceptance met; clean rebuild ctest 3/3.
 - Item1 write(Address,Table): Hdf5File::write — product group at address path (intermediate groups), exact arrow::Schema as IPC blob dataset (__arrow_schema__) + num_rows/schema-metadata attrs; per-column typed datasets (primitives 1-D; fixed_size_list 2-D rectangular; list values+.__offsets__; struct subgroup; list<struct> subgroup+offsets; utf8 vlen strings; large_binary vlen uint8; nulls via .__valid__).
 - Item2 read(Address): reconstructs exact schema from IPC blob, rebuilds arrays (builders for leaves, ArrayData for nested) -> exact type/nullability/metadata round-trip.
 - Item3 scan(): H5Ovisit collects product groups (have __arrow_schema__), Address::from_path each, Hierarchy::from_addresses (numeric sort).
 - Item4 docs/layout.md (convention) — done in skeleton commit.
 - Item5 ctest: test_address, test_hierarchy (pure), test_roundtrip (flat tensor, list trace, nested list<struct> depo, dense fixed_size_list frame; Equals check_metadata=true) + scan assertions. 3/3.
 - Acceptance: builds with only Arrow+HDF5 (no Phlex/WCT); round-trips for flat/list/list<struct>; in-place dense waveform = 2-D H5T_IEEE_F32LE (verified h5dump; h5py absent so used h5dump). 
 Binding: raw HDF5 C API (no HighFive) per design. Memory: arrow-hdf-needs-hdf5-in-view (resolved).
close_reason: arrow-hdf delivered in source/arrow-hdf (commits 53391f5, 46f47d8): pure (Phlex/WCT-free) Arrow<->HDF5 serializer. Neutral Address + reversible path escaping; Hierarchy descriptor; Hdf5File write/read/scan via raw HDF5 C API with schema-driven per-column typed datasets (rectangular 2-D for dense fixed_size_list, values+offsets for lists, subgroups for structs, vlen for strings/blobs, .__valid__ for nulls) + exact arrow::Schema IPC blob for faithful reconstruction; docs/layout.md convention. Acceptance met: builds with only Arrow+HDF5; ctest 3/3 incl. round-trip (Equals check_metadata) for flat/list/list<struct>; dense waveform verified in-place as 2-D H5T_IEEE_F32LE via h5dump (h5py not installed). Binding = HDF5 C API (no HighFive) per design.

title: Await Phlex 0.3.0: new source/driver model
description:
Tracking/blocker for the part of the read path that genuinely requires Phlex's
new source/driver model (expected in 0.3.0): a driver that auto-discovers which
data-cell indices exist in the input file(s) (driver<->source 'collusion'), a
source acting as a factory of MULTIPLE providers, and a driver accepting
MULTIPLE sources.

## Update (2026-06-17) — scope NARROWED

Per assurances from the Phlex developers: 0.3.0 should be ADDITIVE and not break
0.2.0 except for code inside phlex::experimental (and similarly-named 'stay out'
namespaces). 0.2.0 ALREADY supports the single-provider case (PHLEX_REGISTER_PROVIDERS
+ m.provide(lambda(data_cell_index) -> product); drive with the built-in
generate_layers driver). The new source/multi-provider support is expected to be
additive.

Therefore single-provider read-path work may PROCEED NOW on 0.2.0 and was
unblocked from this issue: the read PROVIDER (read one product from one open
HDF5 file given a cell index), the phlex-arrow-common read-side helpers, and the
scan-trie -> cell-hierarchy interpretation. These use the stable m.provide API
and our existing arrow-hdf read()/scan() + from_table_group (c3s.4).

## Still blocked by THIS issue (needs 0.3.0)

- File-driven read DRIVER: driver auto-discovers cells from a file scan so the
  cells it yields coincide with what providers can supply (the 'collusion').
- source-as-factory-of-many-providers; driver accepting multiple sources.
- multi-technology input hierarchy merge/authority.

## Caveat (applies regardless)

All our code (write AND read) builds on phlex::experimental, so the eventual
0.3.0 upgrade carries the general experimental-namespace risk the Phlex team
flagged. This is not specific to the read path and does not change the decision.

## Exit criteria

Close when Phlex 0.3.0 is in the build environment AND the driver/source
collusion mechanism is understood well enough to design the multi-source driver.
notes:
UPDATE 2026-06-18 (driver API investigation, from ddm-c3s.16): the custom-driver registration surface is NOT shipped in the installed Phlex 0.2.0 — phlex/driver.hpp is absent (though #included by framework_graph.hpp/load_module.hpp), and driver_proxy/driver_bundle are only forward-referenced. The driver model that IS present: async_driver<data_cell_index_ptr> generator + data_cell_cursor::yield_child + fixed_hierarchy (neutral (layer,number) yielding). Sources are pure reactors; there is NO provider->driver channel (driver is sole authority on the index spine). So the file-driven read driver (c3s.16) is blocked here specifically on (a) a shippable custom-driver registration API, and possibly (b) whatever collusion/discovery channel the new source/driver model introduces. Recommended target architecture recorded on c3s.16: scan -> neutral CellHierarchy (c3s.15) -> generic hierarchy-driven driver; keep technology scan out of the driver.
RESOLVED (2026-07-01): Phlex 0.3.0 is installed and the new source/driver model is understood (verified against reference/phlex @ v0.3). Findings:
- phlex::source (public alias) with create_providers(product_selector)->provider_bundles (implicit provider factory, called by the framework per downstream selector) + indices()->index_generator (cell enumerator). Registered via PHLEX_REGISTER_SOURCE + add_source<T>.
- A source acts as a factory of MANY providers (create_providers returns a vector) -> the 'source-as-factory-of-many-providers' item is satisfied by the API.
- Driver plugins ship (phlex/driver.hpp): PHLEX_REGISTER_DRIVER; driver functions take (yielder/cursor, SourceT const&...) so the driver receives the CONFIGURED SOURCE OBJECTS -> file-driven driver<->source collusion via a shared live source object.
- Multiple sources per driver are supported (driver fn takes several SourceT args; config names them) -> basis for multi-technology (ddm-c3s.11).
Implementation now tracked by ddm-c3s.18 (adopt phlex::source read path) + ddm-c3s.16 (file-driven driver). Closing this tracker.
close_reason: Closed

title: Phlex Arrow narrow-waist file I/O
description:
Umbrella epic for building Arrow-based file I/O for Phlex using a "narrow waist"
factoring that reduces the N transient-types x M file-technologies serialization
matrix to N + M.

## Background

Phlex graphs emit a variety of transient product types (Wire-Cell Toolkit
abstract `IData` classes, plain structs, Arrow objects). Persistence must target
a variety of file technologies (ROOT TTree, ROOT RNTuple, Apache Parquet,
HDF5). Mapping every transient type to every technology is an N x M matrix.

The design introduces **Apache Arrow as a single intermediate transient
representation** (a narrow waist). Then we need only N converters (transient <->
Arrow, reusable across technologies) and M serializers (Arrow <-> technology,
reusable across types): cost N + M instead of N x M. Arrow is chosen because its
schema is self-describing, so the Arrow <-> technology serializers can be
generic and schema-driven (no per-type code).

Conversion between native types and Arrow is done with **explicit Phlex
transform nodes** in the graph (convert-once, since Phlex stores are immutable).
Each file technology is a **first-class Phlex output module / provider**, which
also avoids the index-flattening defect found in the existing FORM prototype
(an output module can read the full structured `store.index()` directly).

## Full design

The complete analysis lives in `phlex-file-io-design.md` in the ddm umbrella
top directory (the parent of `sources/`). Every child issue should reference it.
Key sections: FORM analysis and shortcomings; N x M -> N + M; why Arrow; the
package factoring; output module / provider duties; driver-provider collusion;
concurrency; escape hatches.

## Package map

Pure (no Phlex):
- `wire-cell-arrow` (exists): WCT IData <-> Arrow converters + reconstructing
  facades (ArrowFrame/ArrowDepo/ArrowTrace).
- `arrow-hdf` (needed): generic Arrow <-> HDF5 serialization.
- later: `arrow-rntuple`, `arrow-parquet`.

Phlex glue:
- `phlex-arrow-common` (needed): Phlex-aware but technology-agnostic helpers.
- `wire-cell-phlex-arrow` (needed): thin to_arrow / from_arrow transform nodes.
- `phlex-arrow-hdf` (needed): thin output module + provider + driver delegating
  to `arrow-hdf`.

## Scaling rule

Adding a technology = one pure `arrow-<tech>` + one thin `phlex-arrow-<tech>`;
no domain or common-package changes. Adding a domain = one `<domain>-arrow` +
one thin `<domain>-phlex-arrow`; no technology-package changes.

## Important constraint

The Phlex team is reworking providers/drivers for release 0.3.0 (a new "source"
class that is a factory of one or more providers; a driver that accepts one or
more sources). All read-path work that touches drivers/providers/source-collusion
is DEFERRED until Phlex >= 0.3.0. Write-path work can proceed now.

title: Decide: channel mask map (CMM) representation in wc.frame schema
description: IFrame::masks() returns ChannelMaskMap = map<string,map<int,vector<pair<int,int>>>>: label -> channel -> list of half-open bin ranges. Must be stored in wc.frame for full round-trip fidelity. Human decision needed before T-SD-FRAME can be finalized.
design:
wc.frame.cmm schema (proposed). IFrame::masks() = Waveform::ChannelMaskMap = map<string label, map<int channel, vector<pair<int,int>> ranges>>, ranges half-open [start,end). Stored as a FLAT companion table in the wc.frame bundle (per ddm-li8 #1 companion-table decision + ddm-ooc flat-columns philosophy).

DECISION (Option 1, fully flat): table wc.frame.cmm, arrow.schema=wc.frame.cmm, one row per (label,channel,range), all columns non-null:
  wc.frame.cmm.label     utf8   (mask label)
  wc.frame.cmm.channel   int32  (channel ident; consistent with wc.trace.channel)
  wc.frame.cmm.bin_start int32  (inclusive  = BinRange.first)
  wc.frame.cmm.bin_end   int32  (exclusive  = BinRange.second; HALF-OPEN, documented to avoid fencepost)
Ordering: ChannelMaskMap/ChannelMasks are std::map (sorted label then channel), BinRangeList ordered vector; emit in iteration order so row order preserves per-(label,channel) range order -> faithful round-trip. EMPTY CMM (common): omit the wc.frame.cmm table from the bundle (missing => empty); always-present-empty table is acceptable alt for schema uniformity.

REJECTED: (2) grouped per (label,channel) with ranges:list<fixed_size_list<int32>[2]> or two aligned lists — fewer rows but reintroduces nesting / two-list alignment hazard; CMM leaf is a pair so grouping costs more structure than it saves (ooc grouped because its leaf was a scalar index); re-group from flat is a trivial group-by. (3) fully nested map<utf8,map<int32,list<struct<int32,int32>>>> — lossless but deeply nested, awkward builders/consumers. (4) JSON-in-metadata — opaque, non-queryable, violates the native-binary/no-JSON principle from ddm-ooc.

NOTE: diverges from this issue's original note only on LOCATION — not a column on the trace table (cardinality mismatch with one-row-per-trace) but its own flat companion table, which is what 'flattened list column' intended.
notes: MEMORY/REPLICATION note (human Q during ddm-ub3 review): the repeated 'label' value is not a concern. Arrow utf8 stores one contiguous values buffer + int32 offsets (no per-row std::string/alloc). 1000 identical 'bad' rows ~= 3000 B values + ~4 KB offsets ~= 7 KB, vs ~12 KB for the 3 int32 columns and ~60 MB for one APA's charge data — negligible. Optional optimization, NOT required: dictionary<int32,utf8> dedups labels in memory (~4 KB), and Parquet dictionary-encodes the utf8 column automatically on write (logical type unchanged). Canonical logical type stays utf8; dictionary encoding is a transparent storage/in-memory option.
close_reason: CMM decision reached and recorded in design field: flat companion table wc.frame.cmm {label:utf8, channel:int32, bin_start:int32, bin_end:int32} (half-open), one row per (label,channel,range); omit table when empty. User-approved in session. Final schema sign-off at ddm-ub3.

title: Decide: IDepo prior() chain representation in wc.depo schema
description: IDepo::prior() returns a shared_ptr to the source depo (e.g. original before drift), forming a linked chain. WCT WireCellSio handles this by assigning sequential IDs and storing the prior ID. Need equivalent in Arrow. Human decision needed before T-SD-DEPO can be finalized.
design:
DECISION (2026-06-12, user-approved): represent IDepo::prior() chains as a NESTED list<struct> per active depo (Option B) — NOT a flat row-index/gen-child scheme.

wc.deposet table: one row per ACTIVE depo, flat per-depo columns (all non-null):
  wc.depo.time float64 (s); wc.depo.charge float64 (#electrons); wc.depo.energy float64 (MeV);
  wc.depo.x/y/z float64; wc.depo.extent_long float64; wc.depo.extent_tran float64;
  wc.depo.id int32 (Geant4 track id); wc.depo.pdg int32 (PDG).
plus the prior chain:
  wc.depo.priors : list<struct<time:f64,charge:f64,energy:f64,x:f64,y:f64,z:f64,extent_long:f64,extent_tran:f64,id:i32,pdg:i32>>
    - ordered MOST-RECENT-FIRST: priors[0]=immediate prior (gen 1), priors[1]=its prior (gen 2), ... (matches depo_chain()).
    - non-nullable list; ZERO-LENGTH when the depo has no prior.
    - struct fields mirror the flat per-depo columns exactly.
Reconstruction: build active SimpleDepo from flat columns; for each, walk priors[] building SimpleDepo and set_prior sequentially (priors[i].prior = priors[i+1]). No index resolution, no gen/child math.

RATIONALE / confirmations from user:
 - The prior chain is a per-active linear history (e.g. drift transform of an 'atomic' depo). USER CONFIRMS the current IDepo application NEVER has two children sharing one prior -> a FOREST, not a DAG -> nesting is exact and loses nothing (Arrow duplication of shared priors is moot). If a DAG ever arises, revisit (would need the self-ref-index model, Option A).
 - Most Arrow-native: prior chain is a first-class nested type, not an index convention; build = walk prior()->append struct; read = sequential link. Eliminates the gen/child packing/unpacking the user wanted gone.
 - Robust under Arrow collection operators (ddm-qof): filtering/sorting/concat of deposet rows carries each depo's provenance with it; no dangling positional refs.
 - ENERGY: WCT numpy fill() drops IDepo::energy(); user notes energy is effectively unused / arguably shouldn't exist on IDepo, but since it IS on the interface we INCLUDE it for full round-trip fidelity.

REJECTED: Option A (single all-depos table + nullable self-ref prior int32 + active bool) — keeps priors as first-class columnar rows (better for columnar pre-drift analysis) and could dedup sharing, but uses positional refs (fragile under row ops) and is just WCT's scheme de-obfuscated. User judged first-class columnar priors not needed in practice. Options C/D (two-table; chain_id+depth grouping) — more moving parts, no benefit.

x/y/z kept as three flat float64 columns (columnar filtering) rather than fixed_size_list<f64>[3]. Units are WCT system-of-units (time s, charge #e, energy MeV, lengths WCT length unit); optional field-level wc.unit metadata at implementation discretion. Feeds ddm-dgc (wc.depo) and ddm-4fb (wc.deposet).
notes: Options: (1) Row-index reference: wc.depo.prior_row int32 = row index of prior in same Table or -1. Simple, efficient, requires full chain in one Table. (2) ID reference: wc.depo.prior_id using IDepo::id() (Geant4 track ID) - may not be unique within a set. (3) Assigned sequential local ID: add wc.depo.local_id and wc.depo.prior_local_id - mirrors WireCellSio, cross-table refs possible. (4) Omit prior - loses round-trip fidelity. Options 1 or 3 recommended; review WireCellSio serialization for prior art.
close_reason: Decision reached and recorded in design field (user-approved): IDepo prior chains encoded as a nested list<struct> of ancestor depos per active depo (Option B), most-recent-first, empty list when none. Forest semantics confirmed by user (no shared priors). energy() restored for fidelity (numpy scheme drops it). Feeds ddm-dgc (wc.depo) and ddm-4fb (wc.deposet).

title: Decide: frame/trace tag representation in wc.frame schema
description: IFrame exposes a tagging system needed for full round-trip fidelity: frame_tags() list of string tags for the whole frame; trace_tags() union of all per-trace tags; tagged_traces(tag) trace indices per tag; trace_summary(tag) optional per-trace double summary per tag. All must be stored in wc.frame. Human decision needed before T-SD-FRAME can be finalized.
design:
DECISION (2026-06-11): Represent IFrame tags with FLAT Arrow columns (no JSON, native binary, summary merged via nullable list).

Three concerns, three native-Arrow homes:

1. frame_tags() — standalone vector<string>, no trace association.
   Table wc.frame.frame_tags, one row per frame tag:
     tag : utf8        (order = row order)

2. trace_tags() / tagged_traces(tag) / trace_summary(tag) — combined into ONE table.
   Table wc.frame.trace_tags, one row per trace tag:
     tag         : utf8
     trace_index : list<int64>      (ordered; raw indices into the full ITrace vector; order may differ from trace vector order and is preserved by list order)
     summary     : list<float64>    (NULLABLE: null = no summary for this tag; non-null => length == this row's trace_index length, aligned element-wise summary[i]<->trace_index[i])

   Notes:
   - summary uses Arrow null (validity bit), NOT NaN/sentinel — absent summaries cost ~1 bit, no space waste.
   - trace_tags() (the union of per-trace tags) is derived as the distinct tag values in this table; not stored separately.
   - trace_index is native int64 usable directly to index traces() — no parsing.

Rationale for FLAT columns over map<utf8,struct<...>>:
   - Lookup is O(n) for both (Arrow Map is List<Struct<k,v>>, not a hash map); few tags/frame so irrelevant.
   - Flat is directly queryable by Arrow compute / DuckDB / SQL / pandas; a map cell is opaque/nested.
   - Simpler C++ builders/accessors (StringBuilder + ListBuilder<Int64Builder> + nullable ListBuilder<DoubleBuilder>) vs nested MapBuilder->StructBuilder->ListBuilders.
   - Map shines as a per-row column when one map per row; but a wc.frame is a STANDALONE Arrow object (trace table rows = traces, frame scalars in schema metadata), not a row in a frameset, so map would degenerate to a 1-row column. If frames are ever packed into a frameset table, revisit (map<utf8,struct<trace_index:list<int64>,summary:list<float64>>> would then be natural).

Schema-metadata key conventions (per wc.frame pattern): set arrow.schema = wc.frame.frame_tags / wc.frame.trace_tags on the respective companion tables.

CMM (ddm-ei3) follows the same companion-table pattern; resolve there independently.
notes:
RESOLVED — decision in design field. Implementation folds these companion tables into wc.frame (ddm-li8).

UPDATE 2026-06-12: ddm-p89 (arrow in container) is now CLOSED — arrow is available and the wire-cell-arrow build/test scaffold is verified, so tag implementation is no longer blocked.

CLARIFICATION (trace index is IMPLICIT): the trace_index : list<int64> values in wc.frame.trace_tags reference the IMPLICIT 0-based row position of each trace in the wc.frame (sparse) / wc.trace trace table, in preserved WCT trace order. Per the ddm-yz3 decision there is NO explicit index column — row position IS the canonical trace index. Therefore converters MUST preserve WCT trace row order on round-trip or these tag references (and tagged_traces/trace_summary alignment) silently break. Consistency note for ddm-ub3 review: trace_index uses int64 (matches WCT trace_list_t = vector<size_t>) while wc.trace.channel/tbin use int32; intentional (size_t semantics for indices), flag if uniformity is preferred — trace counts comfortably fit int32.
close_reason: Decision reached and recorded in design field: flat Arrow columns for frame/trace tags (frame_tags table; trace_tags table with tag:utf8, trace_index:list<int64>, nullable summary:list<float64>). Implementation deferred to ddm-li8, now blocked on ddm-p89 (arrow-cpp in container).

Each requires human review before implementation begins.

Reviews approved: ub3 (trace/frame), 748 (depo), x29 (tensor). All store arrow.schema metadata, prefix fields wc.<type>., support full round-trip. Designs recorded in each issue's design field. Implementation proceeds under ddm-1x9.

title: Decide: channel mask map (CMM) representation in wc.frame schema

notes: Options: (1) Fully nested Arrow types: map<utf8,map<int32,list<struct<int32,int32>>>> - lossless but deeply nested and awkward API. (3) JSON string in schema metadata - simple but opaque and non-queryable. (4) Separate companion table - clean but requires two objects. Option 2 (flattened list column) likely best balance.

title: wire-cell-phlex: model module-level config keys in the boost-config schemas
description:
The *Config schemas (wire_cell_phlex/Config.hpp) currently describe only the executor-side keys (under the "executor" field). Keys that each PHLEX module reads directly in PHLEX_REGISTER_ALGORITHMS/PROVIDERS are NOT modeled, so the exported/generated .libjsonnet factory is incomplete for a full node config.

Unmodeled module-level keys observed so far (frame_filter.cpp):
  input_layer (string, required), input_from (string, default "input"),
  input_suffix (string, default "frame"), use_wire_schema (bool) [already in
  FrameFilterConfig], wire_schema_layer (string, default "job").
Other modules (deposet_*, frame_*_file, fanin) read their own layer/creator/
suffix-style keys directly too — audit each modules/*.cpp.

Goal: extend each <Node>Config to include that node modules own keys (e.g. a
Documented input_layer/input_from/... group) so value_to<NodeConfig> and the
emitted factory cover the COMPLETE user-facing config surface, and the module
reads those values from the parsed struct instead of ad-hoc config.get<>.

Design notes:
 - Decide the JSON shape: keep flat module keys alongside "executor", or group
   them (e.g. { executor:{...}, io:{...} }). Prefer additive/uniform.
 - The module still needs some values (layer/creator/suffix) at registration
   time to wire product_selector/input_family; read them from the parsed
   struct. Watch reject_unknown: the object handed to the node must contain
   only modeled keys (or set reject_unknown=false at the module boundary).
 - Regenerate cfg/schema/*.libjsonnet afterward (blocks/relates to the drift
   guard task).
Relates to epic ddm-vvc (closed).

title: wire-cell-phlex: export config schemas + ship generated .libjsonnet

Depends on discovery macro + config structs + refactor.

title: schema-discovery CLI: dlopen a plugin and emit .libjsonnet factories
description:
Separate small tool (its own devel/ package or a python/ CLI) that:

- dlsym+call each, parse the returned JSON object {module: contents}
- write out <module>.libjsonnet files (or print)
Depends on the export macro (child #1) and at least one plugin exporting (child #3). Scope/impl TBD; can be deferred. Consider whether it belongs in python/ (subprocess nm + ctypes.dlopen) for speed of iteration.
